title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Bills to shield residents from data center costs, plus fresh moratoriums from Warren to Wixom.

lines.text: Good evening, and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's a busy Wednesday night across the state, and tonight's big story is a package of bills that could reshape how data centers get built here.
That's right — lawmakers are trying to make sure residents don't end up paying higher electric bills to support these massive facilities.
Exactly. The legislation introduced today would require data centers to meet new standards on power use, water consumption, and workforce practices.
Why does this matter so much right now? We've seen a wave of local pushback in the last day or two.
The goal is to stop residents from subsidizing the industry's growth through higher utility bills. If it passes, it would change how future campuses get sited across Michigan.
So this isn't just about one project — it's statewide. How did we get to this point where towns are scrambling with moratoriums?
The AI boom has driven huge demand for power and water. Data centers can pull enormous amounts, and utilities have to plan for that.
And if those costs get passed along, regular households feel it in their monthly bills. That's the core worry.
Right. The bills also look at water use, which matters in places where supplies are already stretched.
We've got stories from Warren, Wixom, Freedom Township, and others all pushing back today. Is this legislation a direct response to those local fights?
It lines up with the timing. Governor Whitmer is also urging developers to sign a voluntary pledge to protect ratepayers from higher bills and strained water supplies.
A voluntary pledge on top of the bills — that could set the tone even if the legislation takes time.
Meanwhile, Warren's city council voted today to seek a one-year moratorium on new proposals, citing power demand and noise concerns.
That gives them breathing room to write zoning rules. Macomb County folks are watching to see if neighbors follow.
In Washtenaw County's Freedom Township, residents successfully blocked a billion-dollar project that would have used huge amounts of power and water.
That's a big win for locals protecting property values and township services. Small communities really can shift the trajectory.
Wixom kept its moratorium after rejecting a waiver request, and Solon Township postponed rules that would have limited locations in Kent County.
The debate continues there while current zoning stays in place.
Lenox Township is dealing with recall petitions over rumors of proposals, and Mason County saw another flood at a construction site.
Repeated flooding raises runoff and water quality worries for nearby residents.
Yesterday Mason's mayor survived a recall attempt, and Marysville is eyeing its own one-year freeze.
So the local actions are happening fast. How might the new state bills interact with all these moratoriums?
If passed, the standards would apply statewide and could influence where projects even get proposed in the first place.
What about farmland and towns specifically? These facilities often need large sites.
They can take up significant land, affect local services, and raise questions about long-term economic benefits versus infrastructure strain.
And electric bills — that's the one that hits everyone. How big could the impact be if nothing changes?
Utilities have to build or buy more power capacity. Without protections, those costs can flow to all ratepayers, not just the data centers.
The legislation aims to shift more of that responsibility onto the developers themselves.
Workforce practices are also in the mix — making sure local hiring and training happen.
What happens next with the bills? Do they have broad support?
They were just introduced today, so the real test will be committee hearings and votes in the coming weeks.
In the meantime, towns like Ypsilanti Township are holding informational sessions tonight.
Let's move to quick hits. Warren's council is seeking that one-year halt amid power and noise worries.
Freedom Township residents blocked the big project, protecting their water and power supplies for now.
Whitmer's voluntary pledge is out there too, asking developers to commit to shielding residents.
Wixom rejected the waiver and kept its moratorium in place.
Solon Township delayed its ordinance, so the debate rolls on in Kent County.
Lenox Township faces recall petitions over the rumors, and Mason County residents are alarmed by the second flood at the site.
Yesterday Mason's mayor stayed in office after the clerk tossed recall signatures, and Marysville is looking at its own pause.
That's a lot of local action in just two days.
Now for on deck — meetings listeners can actually attend.
Tonight Ypsilanti Township Board of Trustees has an informational session on data center updates and local impacts.
Also tonight, Augusta Township Planning Commission reviews zoning ordinance changes including data center definitions.
Wixom City Council meets on a data center ordinance amendment for future regulations.
Ypsilanti Township is also holding a session on the University of Michigan/Los Alamos project updates, history, and Q&A.
Tomorrow the Michigan Public Service Commission discusses data center power contracts and rate impacts.
Plenty for folks to weigh in on if these issues hit close to home.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Lawmakers Target Ratepayer Protection as Data Centers Strain Michigan Power and Water
